title: Debugger, sanitizers and final project

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Debugger, sanitizers and final project; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: C++ sanitizers instrument selected undefined, address, or memory behaviors on executed paths and complement type, ownership, lifetime, and contract reasoning.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: AddressSanitizer reports a use-after-free reached by a test, and ownership is redesigned with RAII rather than suppressing the report. Your goal is to deliver and explain a tested multi-file CLI application with no known sanitizer findings. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Conceptual constraints and diagnostics; Exceptions and guarantees; Unit and integration tests; CMake project structure; unique_ptr, shared_ptr and non-owning access. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: C++ sanitizers instrument selected undefined, address, or memory behaviors on executed paths and complement type, ownership, lifetime, and contract reasoning. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: They do not cover all defects, different sanitizers conflict or change timing, optimized and unexecuted paths differ, and clean runs are not proof. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: Passing all sanitizer tests certifies that a C++ program has no lifetime or undefined-behavior defects.

includes: Combine tests, debugger state and sanitizer reports to repair lifetime, bounds and undefined-behavior defects.
Deliver and explain a tested multi-file CLI application with no known sanitizer findings.
excludes: They do not cover all defects, different sanitizers conflict or change timing, optimized and unexecuted paths differ, and clean runs are not proof.

outcomes: Deliver and explain a tested multi-file CLI application with no known sanitizer findings.

examples.title: Debugger, sanitizers and final project: defined case
examples.situation: AddressSanitizer reports a use-after-free reached by a test, and ownership is redesigned with RAII rather than suppressing the report.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: AddressSanitizer reports a use-after-free reached by a test, and ownership is redesigned with RAII rather than suppressing the report.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Sanitizer results are path-limited evidence; combine multiple appropriate tools with static diagnostics, ownership design, invariants, and boundary tests.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Sanitizer results are path-limited evidence; combine multiple appropriate tools with static diagnostics, ownership design, invariants, and boundary tests.

examples.title: Debugger, sanitizers and final project: boundary case
examples.situation: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer exposes signed overflow at a boundary input and the operation gains a precondition or checked arithmetic.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: UndefinedBehaviorSanitizer exposes signed overflow at a boundary input and the operation gains a precondition or checked arithmetic.
Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: They do not cover all defects, different sanitizers conflict or change timing, optimized and unexecuted paths differ, and clean runs are not proof.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: Passing all sanitizer tests certifies that a C++ program has no lifetime or undefined-behavior defects.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Deliver and explain a tested multi-file CLI application with no known sanitizer findings.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: Passing all sanitizer tests certifies that a C++ program has no lifetime or undefined-behavior defects.
misconceptions.correction: Sanitizer results are path-limited evidence; combine multiple appropriate tools with static diagnostics, ownership design, invariants, and boundary tests.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: Passing all sanitizer tests certifies that a C++ program has no lifetime or undefined-behavior defects.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Debugger, sanitizers and final project: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
They do not cover all defects, different sanitizers conflict or change timing, optimized and unexecuted paths differ, and clean runs are not proof.
